title: Space Radiation Testing in the New Space Era: When the Rules Change
script:
The traditional space industry has a simple answer to radiation: test everything, apply twice the design margin, and only use military-spec parts. That approach made sense when launching a billion-dollar GEO satellite. It makes much less sense when you're a New Space startup trying to ship a CubeSat in nine months on a shoestring budget.

Matthew Gill is a PhD nuclear engineer who spent years qualifying radiation-tolerant hardware for companies like Maxar, Airbus, and OneWeb. He has worked on over two hundred flight systems. And he has published something that most radiation engineers won't say out loud: sometimes, skipping formal radiation testing is the right call.

Before we get into that argument, let's calibrate on the environment itself — because not all orbits are equal, and this is where most people's intuition breaks down.

Here are a few things that often surprise engineers new to the field. First, commercial off-the-shelf parts — what the industry calls COTS — tend to fail when they accumulate more than about 25 kilorad of total ionizing dose. That's the threshold where the semiconductor oxide layers start to degrade in ways that affect performance. Second — and this one is genuinely counterintuitive — single-event effect rates at high LEO altitudes above a thousand kilometres can actually be worse than at GEO. Altitude is not monotonically safer. And third, no amount of aluminum shielding will protect you from destructive single-event effects like latch-up. A single high-energy heavy ion can trigger a low-impedance short circuit in a CMOS device regardless of how much metal you wrap around it. De-rating your power devices is one of the few hardware-level mitigations that actually works.

So given all that, when does Gill argue you can skip formal testing? He identifies two scenarios. The first is short demonstration missions — a proof-of-concept payload flying for six months doesn't need the same qualification rigor as a seven-year communications satellite. The TID budget is low, the mission criticality is bounded, and the cost of testing may simply exceed the value of the information you'd get back. Fail fast, learn, and build the tested version next cycle.

The second case is longer missions where the numbers work out without testing. If you've done orbit dose analysis, selected components with known flight heritage, applied reasonable COTS selection rules, and your expected failure probability sits within your risk budget — formal testing may not add enough marginal information to justify the cost. Gill is careful to say this isn't a license to be careless. It's an actuarial engineering framework.

Now, what does the on-orbit failure data actually tell us? Here's an uncomfortable truth: it's almost impossible to get reliable public data on what kills satellites and why. Companies rarely disclose root causes, and even when a failure is observed, attribution is hard. What we do know is that device resets are the dominant early failure mode in LEO — primarily affecting radios and onboard computers. The most cited concrete data point is the Xilinx Zynq-7000 processor, one of the most popular SoCs in the CubeSat world. At 800 kilometres altitude, it experiences approximately three SEU-induced resets per day — SEU standing for single-event upset, a bit flip triggered by a cosmic particle — because it lacks built-in error-correcting memory. Three reboots a day isn't a catastrophic failure, but it means your system is constantly losing state and potentially corrupting data. The fix is well-known: add external ECC, run a memory scrubber, and implement watchdog recovery. Skipping these because you're using commercial parts is an engineering error, not a cost saving.

For programs where full testing really isn't feasible, Gill outlines a hierarchy of alternatives. Use hardware that already has flight history, even without formal test data. Mine the public databases from NSREC and RADECS — those conference proceedings contain test characterization data for hundreds of common components. Avoid complex ICs with no documented radiation history. And apply design-level mitigations like error-correcting codes, watchdog timers, power de-rating, and current limiting.

There's a broader supply chain angle here worth flagging for anyone following Taiwan's position in the LEO ecosystem. Companies like Win Semiconductors and Ascend Technology have clearly passed some form of radiation qualification to sit inside Starlink and Kuiper supply chains — but Taiwan has no domestic heavy-ion accelerator or large cobalt-60 facility capable of running full SEE and TID qualification to MIL-STD-883 standards. Every certification cycle requires a trip to Berkeley, Vancouver, or Germany. For a new entrant trying to qualify a novel component, that's a serious time and cost multiplier that rarely gets discussed.

If you found this useful, subscribe for more engineering deep-dives on space hardware, RF systems, and the infrastructure behind New Space. Links to Matthew Gill's free orbit radiation tools and all sources are in the description below.
